reasoning: The ECB is currently undergoing structural quantitative tightening (QT), running down its APP and PEPP portfolios by around €40 billion per month. This strongly dictates a downward trend over the next 1-2 years. Analyst forecasts (like Bank of America and ING) expect this QT to bottom out around late 2026 or 2027, projecting a trough in the balance sheet between €5.7T and €6.0T. Since our base date (April 2026) is near the €6.2T mark, the balance sheet is very likely to shrink relative to this base value over the next 1-2 years. Short-term (1 week to 1 month), the high weekly volatility of reserves (up to €100 billion swings, e.g., for quarter-end or holidays) keeps the probability closer to a coin toss, though leaning negative. As the 40B/mo trend accumulates, probabilities drop to their lowest by 2027 (the expected trough). In the long term (2029+), the ECB plans to implement structural operations that will likely grow with the nominal size of the banking sector. Assuming a return to steady nominal growth (3-4% per year), the balance sheet will begin to expand again from its 2027 trough, likely reaching or slightly exceeding the 2026 base value by 2031 and beyond. To reflect the required increasing uncertainty at longer horizons, the distant projections are kept cautiously close to 0.50 (e.g., 0.52 and 0.58).
